question:
Let $n \geq 2$ be an integer and $T_n$ be the number of non-empty
subsets $S$ of $\{1, 2, 3, \dots, n\}$ with the property that the
average of the elements of $S$ is an integer. Prove that
$T_n - n$ is always even.
solution:
Note that each of the sets $\{1\}, \{2\}, \dots, \{n\}$ has the
desired property. Moreover, for each set $S$ with integer average $m$
that does not contain $m$, $S \cup \{m\}$ also has average $m$,
while for each set $T$ of more than one element with integer average
$m$ that contains $m$, $T \setminus \{m\}$ also has average $m$.
Thus the subsets other than $\{1\}, \{2\}, \dots, \{n\}$ can be grouped
in pairs, so $T_n - n$ is even.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $upperbound \geq 2$ be an integer and $subsetcount$ be the number of non-empty
subsets $subsetvar$ of $\{1, 2, 3, \dots, upperbound\}$ with the property that the
average of the elements of $subsetvar$ is an integer. Prove that
$subsetcount - upperbound$ is always even.
descriptive_long.solution:
Note that each of the sets $\{1\}, \{2\}, \dots, \{upperbound\}$ has the
desired property. Moreover, for each set $subsetvar$ with integer average $meanvalue$
that does not contain $meanvalue$, $subsetvar \cup \{meanvalue\}$ also has average $meanvalue$,
while for each set $largergrp$ of more than one element with integer average
$meanvalue$ that contains $meanvalue$, $largergrp \setminus \{meanvalue\}$ also has average $meanvalue$.
Thus the subsets other than $\{1\}, \{2\}, \dots, \{upperbound\}$ can be grouped
in pairs, so $subsetcount - upperbound$ is even.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $pavilion \geq 2$ be an integer and $panorama$ be the number of non-empty
subsets $sunflower$ of $\{1, 2, 3, \dots, pavilion\}$ with the property that the
average of the elements of $sunflower$ is an integer. Prove that
$panorama - pavilion$ is always even.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Note that each of the sets $\{1\}, \{2\}, \dots, \{pavilion\}$ has the
desired property. Moreover, for each set $sunflower$ with integer average $waterfall$
that does not contain $waterfall$, $sunflower \cup \{waterfall\}$ also has average $waterfall$,
while for each set $whirlwind$ of more than one element with integer average
$waterfall$ that contains $waterfall$, $whirlwind \setminus \{waterfall\}$ also has average $waterfall$.
Thus the subsets other than $\{1\}, \{2\}, \dots, \{pavilion\}$ can be grouped
in pairs, so $panorama - pavilion$ is even.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $infinite \geq 2$ be an integer and $emptiness$ be the number of non-empty
subsets $superset$ of $\{1, 2, 3, \dots, infinite\}$ with the property that the
average of the elements of $superset$ is an integer. Prove that
$emptiness - infinite$ is always even.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Note that each of the sets $\{1\}, \{2\}, \dots, \{infinite\}$ has the
desired property. Moreover, for each set $superset$ with integer average $variance$
that does not contain $variance$, $superset \cup \{variance\}$ also has average $variance$,
while for each set $universe$ of more than one element with integer average
$variance$ that contains $variance$, $universe \setminus \{variance\}$ also has average $variance$.
Thus the subsets other than $\{1\}, \{2\}, \dots, \{infinite\}$ can be grouped
in pairs, so $emptiness - infinite$ is even.

garbled_string.question:
Let $xgtrmzpl \geq 2$ be an integer and $rckpvasm$ be the number of non-empty
subsets $qzxwvtnp$ of $\{1, 2, 3, \dots, xgtrmzpl\}$ with the property that the
average of the elements of $qzxwvtnp$ is an integer. Prove that
$rckpvasm - xgtrmzpl$ is always even.
garbled_string.solution:
Note that each of the sets $\{1\}, \{2\}, \dots, \{xgtrmzpl\}$ has the
desired property. Moreover, for each set $qzxwvtnp$ with integer average $hjgrksla$
that does not contain $hjgrksla$, $qzxwvtnp \cup \{hjgrksla\}$ also has average $hjgrksla$,
while for each set $vbfjdnqe$ of more than one element with integer average
$hjgrksla$ that contains $hjgrksla$, $vbfjdnqe \setminus \{hjgrksla\}$ also has average $hjgrksla$.
Thus the subsets other than $\{1\}, \{2\}, \dots, \{xgtrmzpl\}$ can be grouped
in pairs, so $rckpvasm - xgtrmzpl$ is even.

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an odd prime $p$ and an integer $d\ge 2$.  
Put  
\[
V=\{0,1,\dots ,p-1\}^{d}\;\simeq\;(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^{d},
\qquad |V|=p^{d},
\]  
and for every non-empty subset $S\subseteq V$ write its vector sum  

\[
\sigma(S)=\sum_{x\in S}x \pmod{p}\qquad(\text{component-wise}).
\]

Let  

\[
\Delta=\{(t,t,\dots ,t)\; :\; t\in\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\}\le (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^{d}
\]

be the $1$-dimensional diagonal subspace and define  

\[
D_{p,d}= \bigl|\{\,S\subseteq V : S\neq\varnothing,\ \sigma(S)\in\Delta \}\bigr|.
\]

(1) Show that the exact closed formula  
\[
\boxed{\;
D_{p,d}=p^{-(d-1)}\Bigl(2^{\,p^{d}}+\bigl(p^{\,d-1}-1\bigr)\,2^{\,p^{d-1}}-p^{\,d-1}\Bigr)
\;}
\tag{F}
\]

holds for every $d\ge 2$.

(2) Deduce the arithmetic consequences  

\[
\boxed{\;
\nu_{p}\!\bigl(D_{p,d}\bigr)=0\quad\text{and}\quad D_{p,d}\equiv 1\pmod{p}}
\]

for all $d\ge 2$.  
In particular $D_{p,2}\equiv 1\pmod{p}$ and for every $d\ge 3$ the
number $D_{p,d}$ is not divisible by $p$ (hence by no higher power of $p$).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout put  
\[
G=(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^{d},\qquad |G|=p^{d},\qquad 
\zeta=e^{2\pi i/p},
\]  
and for $u=(u_{1},\dots ,u_{d})\in G$ define the additive character  

\[
\chi_{u}(x)=\zeta^{\langle u,x\rangle},
\qquad x\in G,
\]  
where $\langle u,x\rangle=u_{1}x_{1}+\dots +u_{d}x_{d}\pmod{p}$.
The family $\{\chi_{u}\}_{u\in G}$ is an orthonormal basis of $\mathbb{C}[G]$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  Fourier expansion of the diagonal indicator  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For a subgroup $H\le G$ and its annihilator
$H^{\perp}:=\{u\in G:\langle u,h\rangle=0\ \forall\,h\in H\}$,
orthogonality gives  

\[
\mathbf 1_{H}(x)=\frac{1}{|H^{\perp}|}\sum_{u\in H^{\perp}}\chi_{u}(x).
\tag{1}
\]

Here \(H=\Delta\) has \(|\Delta|=p\) and therefore  

\[
|\Delta^{\perp}|=\frac{|G|}{|\Delta|}=p^{\,d-1}.
\]

Hence  

\[
\boxed{\;
\mathbf 1_{\Delta}(v)=\frac{1}{p^{\,d-1}}
\sum_{u\in\Delta^{\perp}}\chi_{u}(v)\;} \qquad(v\in G).
\tag{2}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  Transforming the counting problem  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
By definition  

\[
D_{p,d}= \sum_{\varnothing\neq S\subseteq G}\mathbf 1_{\Delta}\bigl(\sigma(S)\bigr)
       =\frac{1}{p^{\,d-1}}
         \sum_{u\in\Delta^{\perp}}
         \sum_{\varnothing\neq S\subseteq G}\chi_{u}\bigl(\sigma(S)\bigr).
\tag{3}
\]

Because \(\chi_{u}\bigl(\sigma(S)\bigr)=\prod_{x\in S}\chi_{u}(x)\), we have  

\[
\sum_{S\subseteq G}\chi_{u}\bigl(\sigma(S)\bigr)=
\prod_{x\in G}\bigl(1+\chi_{u}(x)\bigr)=:P(u).
\tag{4}
\]

(The empty set contributes $1$; subtracting it later eliminates $S=\varnothing$.)  
Thus  

\[
D_{p,d}= \frac{1}{p^{\,d-1}}\sum_{u\in\Delta^{\perp}}\bigl(P(u)-1\bigr).
\tag{5}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Evaluating the products \(P(u)\)  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
(a) \(u=0\):  then \(\chi_{0}\equiv 1\) and  

\[
P(0)=2^{\,|G|}=2^{\,p^{d}}.
\]

(b) \(u\neq 0\):  the multiset \(\{\chi_{u}(x):x\in G\}\) contains every
$p$-th root of unity exactly $p^{\,d-1}$ times, hence  

\[
P(u)=\prod_{k=0}^{p-1}(1+\zeta^{\,k})^{\,p^{\,d-1}}.
\]

Because \(\prod_{k=0}^{p-1}(1+\zeta^{\,k})=2\) (a standard cyclotomic identity),  

\[
\boxed{\;P(u)=2^{\,p^{\,d-1}}\;} 
\qquad(\text{for every }0\neq u\in\Delta^{\perp}).
\tag{6}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  A closed formula for \(D_{p,d}\)  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Since \(|\Delta^{\perp}|=p^{\,d-1}\) and \(\Delta^{\perp}\) has
exactly one zero vector, (5) and (6) give  

\[
\sum_{u\in\Delta^{\perp}}P(u)=2^{\,p^{d}}
      +(p^{\,d-1}-1)\,2^{\,p^{\,d-1}}.
\]

Therefore  

\[
\boxed{\;
D_{p,d}=p^{-(d-1)}\Bigl(2^{\,p^{d}}
 +(p^{\,d-1}-1)\,2^{\,p^{\,d-1}}-p^{\,d-1}\Bigr)},
\tag{F revisited}
\]
which is exactly the claimed formula (F).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.  \(p\)-adic valuation of the numerator  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put  

\[
A:=2^{\,p^{\,d-1}}\qquad\bigl(A\equiv 2\pmod{p}\bigr),
\quad 
B:= 2^{\,p^{d}}+(p^{\,d-1}-1)A-p^{\,d-1}.
\]

Because \(2^{\,p^{d}}=A^{p}\), the bracket can be rewritten as  

\[
B=A^{p}-A+p^{\,d-1}(A-1)
  =(A-1)\bigl(A\sum_{k=0}^{p-2}A^{k}+p^{\,d-1}\bigr).
\tag{7}
\]

(i)  Since \(A\equiv 2\pmod{p}\), we have \(A-1\equiv 1\pmod{p}\); hence  

\[
\nu_{p}(A-1)=0.
\tag{8}
\]

(ii)  To evaluate \(\nu_{p}(A^{p}-A)\) we note
\(A^{p}-A=A(A^{p-1}-1)\) and use the Lifting-The-Exponent lemma:

\[
\nu_{p}(A^{p}-A)=\nu_{p}(2^{\,p^{d}}-2^{\,p^{d-1}})
               =\nu_{p}(2^{\,p-1}-1)+\nu_{p}(p^{\,d-1})
               =(d-1)+w,
\tag{9}
\]
where \(w:=\nu_{p}(2^{\,p-1}-1)\ge 1\) (with \(w=1\) for non-Wieferich primes).

(iii)  The second summand in (7) has  

\[
\nu_{p}\bigl(p^{\,d-1}(A-1)\bigr)=(d-1)+0=d-1.
\tag{10}
\]

Since \(d-1 < w+d-1\), the smaller $p$-adic order in (7) is always \(d-1\); consequently  

\[
\boxed{\;\nu_{p}(B)=d-1\;}.
\tag{11}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 6.  Finishing the valuation  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
From (F) we have \(D_{p,d}=p^{-(d-1)}B\).  Using (11),

\[
\nu_{p}\bigl(D_{p,d}\bigr)=\nu_{p}(B)-(d-1)=0.
\]

Hence \(p\nmid D_{p,d}\).
Moreover, dividing (7) by \(p^{\,d-1}\) we get  

\[
D_{p,d}\equiv (A-1)\equiv 1\pmod{p},
\]

because \(A\equiv 2\pmod{p}\).  
This proves the claimed congruence and completes the solution. \blacksquare 

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original one–dimensional problem, the enhanced variant
introduces several layers of additional technical complexity.

1. Higher-dimensional ambient set  
   – We work in an nᵈ lattice with d ≥ 2, not in a single interval.

2. Constrained centroid geometry  
   – The centroid must be the full diagonal point (m,m,…,m); this couples
     all d coordinate–sums simultaneously and forces the solver to control
     the interaction of several variables at once.

3. Non-trivial verification of centroid preservation  
   – Showing that the simple add/remove operation keeps *all* coordinates
     of the centroid equal requires vector calculus and careful use of the
     centroid condition in d dimensions.

4. Partition of the solution space by centroid value  
   – Unlike the original statement, there are n⁰ rather than n possible
     centroids, and the proof must show the pairing mechanism works
     independently inside each fibre.

These extra structural demands require the solver to juggle several
interacting concepts—vector sums, multi-coordinate means, and centroid
fibre decompositions—making the argument substantially more intricate than
the original pairing in one dimension.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an odd prime $p$ and an integer $d\ge 2$.  
Put  
\[
V=\{0,1,\dots ,p-1\}^{d}\;\simeq\;(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^{d},
\qquad |V|=p^{d},
\]  
and for every non-empty subset $S\subseteq V$ write its vector sum  

\[
\sigma(S)=\sum_{x\in S}x \pmod{p}\qquad(\text{component-wise}).
\]

Let  

\[
\Delta=\{(t,t,\dots ,t)\; :\; t\in\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\}\le (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^{d}
\]

be the $1$-dimensional diagonal subspace and define  

\[
D_{p,d}= \bigl|\{\,S\subseteq V : S\neq\varnothing,\ \sigma(S)\in\Delta \}\bigr|.
\]

(1) Show that the exact closed formula  
\[
\boxed{\;
D_{p,d}=p^{-(d-1)}\Bigl(2^{\,p^{d}}+\bigl(p^{\,d-1}-1\bigr)\,2^{\,p^{d-1}}-p^{\,d-1}\Bigr)
\;}
\tag{F}
\]

holds for every $d\ge 2$.

(2) Deduce the arithmetic consequences  

\[
\boxed{\;
\nu_{p}\!\bigl(D_{p,d}\bigr)=0\quad\text{and}\quad D_{p,d}\equiv 1\pmod{p}}
\]

for all $d\ge 2$.  
In particular $D_{p,2}\equiv 1\pmod{p}$ and for every $d\ge 3$ the
number $D_{p,d}$ is not divisible by $p$ (hence by no higher power of $p$).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout put  
\[
G=(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^{d},\qquad |G|=p^{d},\qquad 
\zeta=e^{2\pi i/p},
\]  
and for $u=(u_{1},\dots ,u_{d})\in G$ define the additive character  

\[
\chi_{u}(x)=\zeta^{\langle u,x\rangle},
\qquad x\in G,
\]  
where $\langle u,x\rangle=u_{1}x_{1}+\dots +u_{d}x_{d}\pmod{p}$.
The family $\{\chi_{u}\}_{u\in G}$ is an orthonormal basis of $\mathbb{C}[G]$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  Fourier expansion of the diagonal indicator  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For a subgroup $H\le G$ and its annihilator
$H^{\perp}:=\{u\in G:\langle u,h\rangle=0\ \forall\,h\in H\}$,
orthogonality gives  

\[
\mathbf 1_{H}(x)=\frac{1}{|H^{\perp}|}\sum_{u\in H^{\perp}}\chi_{u}(x).
\tag{1}
\]

Here \(H=\Delta\) has \(|\Delta|=p\) and therefore  

\[
|\Delta^{\perp}|=\frac{|G|}{|\Delta|}=p^{\,d-1}.
\]

Hence  

\[
\boxed{\;
\mathbf 1_{\Delta}(v)=\frac{1}{p^{\,d-1}}
\sum_{u\in\Delta^{\perp}}\chi_{u}(v)\;} \qquad(v\in G).
\tag{2}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  Transforming the counting problem  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
By definition  

\[
D_{p,d}= \sum_{\varnothing\neq S\subseteq G}\mathbf 1_{\Delta}\bigl(\sigma(S)\bigr)
       =\frac{1}{p^{\,d-1}}
         \sum_{u\in\Delta^{\perp}}
         \sum_{\varnothing\neq S\subseteq G}\chi_{u}\bigl(\sigma(S)\bigr).
\tag{3}
\]

Because \(\chi_{u}\bigl(\sigma(S)\bigr)=\prod_{x\in S}\chi_{u}(x)\), we have  

\[
\sum_{S\subseteq G}\chi_{u}\bigl(\sigma(S)\bigr)=
\prod_{x\in G}\bigl(1+\chi_{u}(x)\bigr)=:P(u).
\tag{4}
\]

(The empty set contributes $1$; subtracting it later eliminates $S=\varnothing$.)  
Thus  

\[
D_{p,d}= \frac{1}{p^{\,d-1}}\sum_{u\in\Delta^{\perp}}\bigl(P(u)-1\bigr).
\tag{5}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Evaluating the products \(P(u)\)  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
(a) \(u=0\):  then \(\chi_{0}\equiv 1\) and  

\[
P(0)=2^{\,|G|}=2^{\,p^{d}}.
\]

(b) \(u\neq 0\):  the multiset \(\{\chi_{u}(x):x\in G\}\) contains every
$p$-th root of unity exactly $p^{\,d-1}$ times, hence  

\[
P(u)=\prod_{k=0}^{p-1}(1+\zeta^{\,k})^{\,p^{\,d-1}}.
\]

Because \(\prod_{k=0}^{p-1}(1+\zeta^{\,k})=2\) (a standard cyclotomic identity),  

\[
\boxed{\;P(u)=2^{\,p^{\,d-1}}\;} 
\qquad(\text{for every }0\neq u\in\Delta^{\perp}).
\tag{6}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  A closed formula for \(D_{p,d}\)  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Since \(|\Delta^{\perp}|=p^{\,d-1}\) and \(\Delta^{\perp}\) has
exactly one zero vector, (5) and (6) give  

\[
\sum_{u\in\Delta^{\perp}}P(u)=2^{\,p^{d}}
      +(p^{\,d-1}-1)\,2^{\,p^{\,d-1}}.
\]

Therefore  

\[
\boxed{\;
D_{p,d}=p^{-(d-1)}\Bigl(2^{\,p^{d}}
 +(p^{\,d-1}-1)\,2^{\,p^{\,d-1}}-p^{\,d-1}\Bigr)},
\tag{F revisited}
\]
which is exactly the claimed formula (F).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5.  \(p\)-adic valuation of the numerator  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put  

\[
A:=2^{\,p^{\,d-1}}\qquad\bigl(A\equiv 2\pmod{p}\bigr),
\quad 
B:= 2^{\,p^{d}}+(p^{\,d-1}-1)A-p^{\,d-1}.
\]

Because \(2^{\,p^{d}}=A^{p}\), the bracket can be rewritten as  

\[
B=A^{p}-A+p^{\,d-1}(A-1)
  =(A-1)\bigl(A\sum_{k=0}^{p-2}A^{k}+p^{\,d-1}\bigr).
\tag{7}
\]

(i)  Since \(A\equiv 2\pmod{p}\), we have \(A-1\equiv 1\pmod{p}\); hence  

\[
\nu_{p}(A-1)=0.
\tag{8}
\]

(ii)  To evaluate \(\nu_{p}(A^{p}-A)\) we note
\(A^{p}-A=A(A^{p-1}-1)\) and use the Lifting-The-Exponent lemma:

\[
\nu_{p}(A^{p}-A)=\nu_{p}(2^{\,p^{d}}-2^{\,p^{d-1}})
               =\nu_{p}(2^{\,p-1}-1)+\nu_{p}(p^{\,d-1})
               =(d-1)+w,
\tag{9}
\]
where \(w:=\nu_{p}(2^{\,p-1}-1)\ge 1\) (with \(w=1\) for non-Wieferich primes).

(iii)  The second summand in (7) has  

\[
\nu_{p}\bigl(p^{\,d-1}(A-1)\bigr)=(d-1)+0=d-1.
\tag{10}
\]

Since \(d-1 < w+d-1\), the smaller $p$-adic order in (7) is always \(d-1\); consequently  

\[
\boxed{\;\nu_{p}(B)=d-1\;}.
\tag{11}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 6.  Finishing the valuation  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
From (F) we have \(D_{p,d}=p^{-(d-1)}B\).  Using (11),

\[
\nu_{p}\bigl(D_{p,d}\bigr)=\nu_{p}(B)-(d-1)=0.
\]

Hence \(p\nmid D_{p,d}\).
Moreover, dividing (7) by \(p^{\,d-1}\) we get  

\[
D_{p,d}\equiv (A-1)\equiv 1\pmod{p},
\]

because \(A\equiv 2\pmod{p}\).  
This proves the claimed congruence and completes the solution. \blacksquare 

--------------------------------------------------------------------
